flags.concern: Social publishing: Legacy `shared_posts` compatibility collides with multi-action post/reply/retweet publishing.
flags.evidence: No flag pointed at the wrong subsystem, so the plan still targets the right code. The revision changes the root-cause treatment instead: `social_publications` becomes canonical, `shared_posts` is explicitly reduced to a legacy compatibility surface, queue claiming is moved into a checked-in DB-atomic SQL/RPC primitive, caller/source context is promoted into the normalized request so reaction moderation and admin defaults are preserved, summary and notification/delete paths are pulled into scope, and the admin/operator surface now includes scheduling and publication visibility instead of only the old Twitter-only tool contract.

flags.concern: Worker lifecycle: Recurring debt: scheduled worker startup is still ambiguous against the repository's real `SharingCog` load path.
flags.evidence: No open flag points to the wrong subsystem or a wrong root-cause diagnosis. The plan still belongs in the sharing stack, but it now closes the two remaining design holes explicitly: the scheduler is tied to the active `SharingCog` lifecycle used by `main.py` instead of the dead-end `setup()` path, and first-share notification and delete flows now carry a canonical `publication_id` so deletion targets one `social_publications` record even when a Discord message has multiple publications.

flags.concern: Are the proposed changes technically correct?: Checked db_handler.py write patterns: the codebase uses both execute_query (raw SQL via supabase_query_handler) and direct Supabase client fluent calls (e.g., .table().upsert().execute()). The plan proposes RPC for atomic claiming but doesn't clarify whether normal CRUD on social_publications will use the fluent client pattern or raw SQL. Both work, but mixing three access patterns (fluent, raw SQL, RPC) in one subsystem increases maintenance surface.
flags.evidence: Checked db_handler.py write patterns: the codebase uses both execute_query (raw SQL via supabase_query_handler) and direct Supabase client fluent calls (e.g., .table().upsert().execute()). The plan proposes RPC for atomic claiming but doesn't clarify whether normal CRUD on social_publications will use the fluent client pattern or raw SQL. Both work, but mixing three access patterns (fluent, raw SQL, RPC) in one subsystem increases maintenance surface.

flags.concern: Does the change touch all locations AND supporting infrastructure?: The admin agent system prompt in agent.py line 38 lists queryable tables including shared_posts. The plan Step 6.5-6.6 covers updating tool descriptions and prompt text, but doesn't explicitly mention adding social_publications and social_channel_routes to the query_table whitelist in tools.py (line 70 and 1637). Missing these would make the new tables invisible to the admin agent.
flags.evidence: The admin agent system prompt in agent.py line 38 lists queryable tables including shared_posts. The plan Step 6.5-6.6 covers updating tool descriptions and prompt text, but doesn't explicitly mention adding social_publications and social_channel_routes to the query_table whitelist in tools.py (line 70 and 1637). flags.concern: DB access patterns: Plan introduces Supabase RPC as a third data access pattern (alongside fluent client and raw SQL) without precedent in the codebase. The RPC function must be created in the Supabase dashboard/migrations before the claim primitive works, and this operational step is not called out.
flags.evidence: Zero .rpc() calls exist in the codebase today (searched src/common/). The plan assumes RPC availability in Step 1.3 but the assumption list only says 'Supabase RPC is available' without noting that it requires server-side function deployment.

flags.concern: Route management: social_channel_routes table is introduced in Step 1 but no admin command, migration, or seeding step is described for populating routes. The routing system will be inert until routes exist.
flags.evidence: Step 5.1 describes resolution logic (channel -> parent -> guild default) but no step covers creating or managing route entries. The admin tool extensions in Step 6 cover read/inspect but not route CRUD.

flags.concern: Did the work fully address the issue hints, user notes, and approved plan requirements?: The plan's assumption that Supabase RPC is available is stated but the codebase has zero existing RPC usage. While the supabase>=1.0.0 client does support `.rpc()`, this is an untested integration pattern in this project and may require additional Supabase dashboard configuration (creating the SQL function). The plan does not call out this operational prerequisite explicitly.
flags.evidence: The plan's assumption that Supabase RPC is available is stated but the codebase has zero existing RPC usage. While the supabase>=1.0.0 client does support `.rpc()`, this is an untested integration pattern in this project and may require additional Supabase dashboard configuration (creating the SQL function). The plan does not call out this operational prerequisite explicitly.

flags.concern: Search for related code that handles the same concept. Is the reported issue a symptom of something broader?: The plan introduces a new social_channel_routes table for per-channel routing configuration, but does not specify how routes are initially populated or managed. There's no admin command or migration step described for creating route entries, which means the routing system will be inert until someone manually inserts rows. The plan should clarify whether guild-default routing is the only first-release path or whether route management tooling is in scope.
